concepts.title: Salt bridge
concepts.conceptBody:
The salt bridge 

(a) acts as a link between the two aqueous solution.

(b) overcomes liquid junction potential.

(c) Maintains the electrical neutrality of the aqueous solution of the electrodes by releasing or sending oppositely charged ions into the solution.

In general $\text{KCl}$ salt bridge is used while $\text{KN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ salt bridge is used when silver electrode is involved as one of the electrodes.

concepts.title: Concentration cells
concepts.conceptBody:
Concentration Cells

The cells whose ${{\mathbf{E}}_{\mathbf{cell }}}$ is zero are called concentration cells. This means that the two compartments (cathode and anode) of the electrochemical cell involves same chemical species but the concentrations of the chemical species in the two compartments are different. The concentration cells are of basically two types.

(a) Electrode concentration cells and

(b) Electrolyte concentration cells

concepts.title: Electrode Concentration Cells
concepts.conceptBody:
Electrode Concentration Cells

In such cells, two similar electrodes at different concentrations/pressures are dipped in the same solution with similar concentration. Let us have an electrochemical cell represented as 
Pt | ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\left( {{\text{P}}_{1}} \right.$ atm ) । ${{\text{H}}^{+}}(\text{cM})$ । । ${{\text{H}}^{+}}(\text{cM})$ । ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\left( {{\text{P}}_{2}} \right.$ atm ) । $\text{Pt}$ For the given cell, the reactions occurring are

At cathode: $2\text{H}_{\text{C}}^{+}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\left( {{\text{P}}_{2}} \right)$

At anode: ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\left( {{\text{P}}_{1}} \right)\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{H}_{\text{A}}^{+}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}$

Net cell reaction: $\overline{\underline{{{\text{H}}_{2}}\left( {{\text{P}}_{1}} \right)\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\left( {{\text{P}}_{2}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,}}$

Since the ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$concentration at the anode and cathode are same, so the net reaction is independent of the concentration of the electrolyte. Applying Nernst equation to the net cell reaction gives

${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}\left( {{25}^{{}^\circ }}\text{C} \right)=\text{E}_{\text{H}_{\text{C}}^{+}\mid {{\text{H}}_{2}}\left( {{\text{P}}_{2}} \right)}^{{}^\circ }-\text{E}_{\text{H}_{\text{A}}^{+}\mid {{\text{H}}_{2}}\left( {{\text{P}}_{1}} \right)}^{{}^\circ }-\frac{0.059}{2}\log \left( \frac{{{\text{P}}_{2}}}{{{\text{P}}_{1}}} \right)$

${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}\left( {{25}^{{}^\circ }}\text{C} \right)=\text{E}_{\text{cell }}^{{}^\circ }-\frac{0.059}{2}\log \left( \frac{{{\text{P}}_{2}}}{{{\text{P}}_{1}}} \right)$

or ${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}\left( {{25}^{{}^\circ }}\text{C} \right)=-\frac{0.059}{2}\log \left( \frac{{{\text{P}}_{2}}}{{{\text{P}}_{1}}} \right)\quad \left( \text{ since, }{{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}=0 \right.\text{ ) }$

${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}\left( {{25}^{{}^\circ }}\text{C} \right)=\frac{0.059}{2}\log \left( \frac{{{\text{P}}_{1}}}{{{\text{P}}_{2}}} \right)$

The EMF of the given cell would be positive when ${{P}_{1}}>{{P}_{2}}$ and the cell reaction would be spontaneous.

Another example of the electrode concentration cell is that of an amalgam with two different concentrations of the same metal dipped in same electrolyte solution.

The cell is represented as

$\text{Hg}-\text{Pb}\left( {{\text{c}}_{1}}\text{M} \right)\left| \text{PbS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}(\text{cM}) \right|\text{Hg}-\text{Pb}\left( {{\text{c}}_{2}}\text{M} \right)$

The reactions for the given cell are

At cathode: \[\text{P}{{\text{b}}^{2+}}(\text{c})+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{Pb}\left( {{\text{c}}_{2}} \right)\]

At anode: $\underline{\text{Pb}\left( {{\text{c}}_{1}} \right)\xrightarrow{{}}\text{P}{{\text{b}}^{2+}}(\text{c})+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}}$

Net cell reaction: $\text{Pb}\left( {{\text{c}}_{1}} \right)\xrightarrow{{}}\text{Pb}\left( {{\text{c}}_{2}} \right)$

Since, the concentration of $\text{P}{{\text{b}}^{2+}}$ for the two half cells is same as the electrolyte solution for the two compartments is same, so the net reaction is independent of the electrolyte concentration. Applying Nernst equation to the net cell reaction gives

${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}(25\text{C})=\text{E}_{\text{P}{{\text{b}}^{2+}}(\text{c})\text{Pb}\left( {{\text{c}}_{2}} \right)}^{{}^\circ }-\text{E}{{_{\text{Pb}}^{{}^\circ }}^{2+(\text{c}))\text{Pb}\left( {{\text{c}}_{1}} \right)}}-\frac{0.059}{2}\log \frac{{{\text{c}}_{2}}}{{{\text{c}}_{1}}}$

${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}(25\text{C})={{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}-\frac{0.059}{2}\log \frac{{{\text{c}}_{2}}}{{{\text{c}}_{1}}}$

\[{{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}(25\text{C})=-\frac{0.059}{2}\log \frac{{{\text{c}}_{2}}}{{{\text{c}}_{1}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\text{(since }{{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}=0)\]

${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}(25\text{C})=-\frac{0.059}{2}\log \frac{{{\text{c}}_{1}}}{{{\text{c}}_{2}}}$

The net cell reaction would be spontaneous, when the EMF of the cell is positive, which is possible only when ${{c}_{1}}>{{c}_{2}}$.

concepts.title: Electrolyte Concentration Cells
concepts.conceptBody:
Electrolyte Concentration Cells

In such cells, two electrodes of the same metal are dipped in solutions of metal ions of different concentrations. Let us have an electrochemical cell represented as

$\text{Pt}\mid {{\text{H}}_{2}}(\text{P}$ atm ) | $\text{HA}\left( {{\text{c}}_{1}}\text{M} \right)$ | । HB $\left( {{\text{c}}_{2}}\text{M} \right)$ | ${{\text{H}}_{2}}(\text{P}$ atm ) | $\text{Pt}$

In such cells, $\text{HA}$ and $\text{HB}$ would represent strong acids, if their ${{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}$ ‘s are not given while they would be weak acids, if their ${{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}$ ‘s are mentioned.

For the given cell, the reactions occurring are

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} At\text{ cathode:} & 2H_{c}^{+}({{c}_{2}})+2{{e}^{-}}\to {{H}_{2}}(P) \\ At\,\,anode: & {{H}_{2}}(P)\to 2H_{A}^{+}({{c}_{1}})+2{{e}^{-}} \\ \text{Net cell reaction:} & \overline{2H_{c}^{+}\to 2H_{A}^{+}\,(n=2)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,} \\ \end{array}$

or $\quad \text{H}_{\text{c}}^{+}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{H}_{\text{A}}^{+}(\text{n}=1)$

The net cell reaction is independent of the pressure terms as the pressure of ${{\text{H}}_{2}}$ in the two half cells is same.

Applying Nernst equation to the net cell reaction gives

${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}(25\text{C})=\text{E}_{\text{H}_{\text{C}}^{+}\mid {{\text{H}}_{2}}}^{{}^\circ }-\text{E}_{\text{H}_{\text{A}}^{+}\mid {{\text{H}}_{2}}}^{{}^\circ }-\frac{0.059}{1}\log \frac{\left[ \text{H}_{\text{A}}^{+} \right]}{\left[ \text{H}_{\text{C}}^{+} \right]}={{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}-0.059\log \frac{{{\text{c}}_{1}}}{{{\text{c}}_{2}}}$

${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}(25\text{C})=0.059\log \frac{{{\text{c}}_{2}}}{{{\text{c}}_{1}}}\text{ (since }{{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}=0\text{ ) }$

The net cell reaction would be feasible spontaneously only when the EMF of the cell is positive, which is possible only when concentration of $\text{H}+$ in cathode compartment $\left( {{\text{c}}_{2}} \right)$ is greater than the concentration of ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$in anode compartment $\left( {{\text{c}}_{1}} \right)$.

concepts.title: Choosing an oxidising agent
concepts.conceptBody:
Selection of an oxidising or reducing agent

Choosing an oxidising agent

It is easier to explain this with a specific example. What could you use to oxidise iron (II) ions to iron (III) ions? The $\text{E}$ value for this reaction is:

\(\mathrm{Fe}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{3+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \quad \mathrm{Fe}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{2+} \mathrm{E}=+0.77 \mathrm{~V}\)

To change iron (II) ions into iron (III) ions, you need to persuade this equilibrium to move to the left. That means that when you couple it to a second equilibrium, this iron E value must be the more negative (less positive one).

You could use anything which has a more positive E value.

Dilute nitric acid:

$\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{3(\text{aq})}}+4\text{H}_{(\text{aq})}^{+}+3{{\text{e}}^{-}}\rightleftharpoons \text{N}{{\text{O}}_{(\text{g})}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{(\text{I})}}\text{E}=+0.96\text{v}$

Acidified potassium dichromate(VI):

$\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}{{^{2-}}_{(\text{aq})}}+14{{\text{H}}^{+}}_{(\text{aq})}+6{{\text{e}}^{-}}\rightleftharpoons 2\text{C}{{\text{r}}^{3+}}_{(\text{aq})}+7{{\text{H}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{(\text{I})}}\text{E}=+1.33\text{v}$

Chlorine:

$\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2(~\text{g})}}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}\rightleftharpoons 2\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}_{(\text{aq})}\text{E}=+1.36\text{v}$

Acidified potassium managanate (VII) :

$\text{MnO}_{4(\text{aq})}^{-}+8\text{H}_{(\text{aq})}^{+}+5{{\text{e}}^{-}}\rightleftharpoons \text{Mn}_{(\text{aq})}^{2+}+4{{\text{H}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{(\text{I})}}\text{E}=+1.51\text{v}$

concepts.title: Choosing a reducing agent
concepts.conceptBody:
Choosing a reducing agent

Remember :

  • Reduction is gain of electrons.
  • A reducing agent reduces something by giving electrons to it. That means that the reducing agent loses electrons.

You have to be a little bit more careful this time, because the substance losing electrons is found on the right-hand side of one of theses redox equilibria. Again, a specific example makes it clearer.

For example, what could you use to reduce chromium (III) ions to chromium (II) ions ?

The E value is:

$\text{C}{{\text{r}}^{3+}}_{(\text{aq})}+{{\text{e}}^{-}}\rightleftharpoons \text{C}{{\text{r}}^{2+}}_{(\text{aq})}\text{E}=0.41\text{v}$

You need this equilibrium to move to the right. That means that when you couple it with a second equilibrium, this chromium $\text{E}$ value must be the most positive (least negative).

In principle, you could choose anything with a more negative $\text{E}$ value - for example, zinc:

$\text{Zn}_{(\text{aq})}^{2+}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}\rightleftharpoons \text{Zn}(\text{s})\text{E}=-0.76\text{v}$

You would have to remember to start from metallic zinc, and not zinc ions. You need this second equilibrium to be able to move to the left to provide the electrons. If you started with zinc ions, it would already be on the left - and would have no electrons to give away. Nothing could possibly happen if you mixed chromium (III) ions and zinc ions.

That is fairly obvious in this simple case. If you were dealing with a more complicated equilibrium, you would have to be careful to think it through properly.
